content.full_text:
September 16, 1975


Mr. Frank J. Burns, Jr.
Attorney at Law
700 South Flower Street, Suite 600
Los Angeles, California 90017

Dear Mr. Burns:

Thank you for your letter of September 2, 1975, re-
questing written confirmation with respect to whether
certain activities in which you engage constitute lobbying
under the Political Reform Act. A lobbyist is defined in
the Political Reform Act as --

... any person who is employed or contracts for
economic consideration, other than reimbursement
for reasonable travel expenses, to communicate
directly or through his agents with any elective
state official, agency official or legislative
official for the purpose of influencing legisla-
tion or administrative action, if a substantial
or regular portion of the activities for which
he receives consideration is for the purpose of
influencing legislation or administrative action.
No person is a lobbyist by reason of activities
described in Section 86300.

This definition has been clarified by the Commission through
the promulgation of Regulation Section 18239, a copy of
which is attached hereto.

The activities you describe in your letter would not
appear to constitute lobbying under the foregoing defini-
tion. They seem to relate primarily to litigation and
providing legal advice to clients, and do not seem to involve
"influencing legislation or administrative action". Accord-
ingly, these activities would not require registration as a
lobbyist.

I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any

Mr. Frank J. Burns, Jr.    2                    September 16, 1975


further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.
                                       Very truly yours,


                                       Kenneth Finney
                                       Director, Legal Department
KF:gp
Enc.

FRANK J. BURNS, JR.
ATTORNEY AT LAW
SUITE 800 BROADWAY PLAZA
750 SOUTH FLOWER STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
TELEPHONE (213) 628-492

September 2, 1975

Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Bennett:

I have carefully reviewed your recently adopted regulation dealing with Quasi-Legislative Administrative Action and have discussed the same with your staff counsel. I have been advised that the activities set forth below do not constitute lobbying and do not require registration. I have also been advised that I could write you to obtain written confirmation thereof.

1. I represent the City of Long Beach in its capacity as Trustee under the Tidelands Trust. The city in such capacity has commenced legal action against various oil companies concerning the price of oil. I have and will continue to have contact with the State Lands Commission and it's staff, the Governor's office, the Attorney General's office, various members of the Legislature, and other governmental officials concerning such litigation. It is my understanding that such activities do not constitute lobbying.

2. I represent Californians for Better Transportation and Citizens for Better Transportation which were campaign committees organized to promote the rapid transit proposals on the June and November ballots of 1974. The Department of Benefit Payments is making certain claims against these organizations. Appropriate administrative appeals have been filed, and the matters are now pending before the Unemployment Insurance Appeals Board. I have and will continue to have contact with various people in the administration in an attempt to have these claims withdrawn as contrary to the law. I understand that such activities do not constitute lobbying.

Mr. Michael Bennett,
Executive Director
Page Two...                                    September 2, 1975


     3.  I have recently been requested to advise AncorD
in connection with the possibility of the sale of certain
property to the state.  I have not as yet contacted anyone,
but I understand that such contact would not constitute
lobbying.

     I would appreciate receiving written confirmation
of my understanding regarding the above matters at your
earliest convenience as I am proceeding on the assumption
that I have been correctly informed.  Should you or any
member of your staff require further information con-
cerning the same, please do not hesitate to contact me.

                                        Yours very truly,

                                        [signature]

                                        FRANK J. BURNS, JR.

FJB:dg

sections.question: Whether certain activities in which you engage constitute lobbying under the Political Reform Act. The activities described include: (1) representing the City of Long Beach as Trustee under the Tidelands Trust in legal action against oil companies, involving contact with State Lands Commission, Governor's office, Attorney General's office, and Legislature members; (2) representing campaign committees (Californians for Better Transportation and Citizens for Better Transportation) in administrative appeals regarding Department of Benefit Payments claims; and (3) advising Ancorp regarding potential sale of property to the state.
sections.conclusion: The activities you describe in your letter would not appear to constitute lobbying under the foregoing definition. They seem to relate primarily to litigation and providing legal advice to clients, and do not seem to involve "influencing legislation or administrative action". Accordingly, these activities would not require registration as a lobbyist.
sections.question_synthetic: Do the activities described—litigation representation for the City of Long Beach, administrative appeals for campaign committees, and advisory work regarding property sales to the state—constitute lobbying requiring registration under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the described activities do not constitute lobbying because they primarily involve litigation and legal advice rather than influencing legislation or administrative action, and therefore registration as a lobbyist is not required.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated September 2, 1975) and FPPC response letter (dated September 16, 1975). The question and conclusion sections are clearly identifiable from the FPPC response portion. The definition of lobbyist is quoted in the response. Response signed by Kenneth Finney, Director of Legal Department.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether certain activities in which you engage constitute lobbying under the Political Reform Act. The activities described include: (1) representing the City of Long Beach as Trustee under the Tidelands Trust in legal action against oil companies, involving contact with State Lands Commission, Governor's office, Attorney General's office, and Legislature members; (2) representing campaign committees (Californians for Better Transportation and Citizens for Better Transportation) in administrative appeals regarding Department of Benefit Payments claims; and (3) advising Ancorp regarding potential sale of property to the state.

CONCLUSION: The activities you describe in your letter would not appear to constitute lobbying under the foregoing definition. They seem to relate primarily to litigation and providing legal advice to clients, and do not seem to involve "influencing legislation or administrative action". Accordingly, these activities would not require registration as a lobbyist.

embedding.summary: FPPC responded to Attorney Frank J. Burns, Jr. advising that his activities representing the City of Long Beach in tidelands litigation, handling administrative appeals for campaign committees, and providing advisory services regarding property sales do not constitute lobbying under the Political Reform Act and do not require lobbyist registration.
